oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object routed by action) -> stdout exactly matching public_test_cases expected_output

trigger_keywords: what prefix to use if none provided
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default prefix is 'notifications.argoproj.io'. You can override this by explicitly passing a prefix argument, but if omitted, this literal string is used.

trigger_keywords: how does the system handle misspelled prefixes
what happens if prefix contains typos
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There is a deliberate typo in the constraint text for C018. The literal string in the PRD is 'notifications.argoroj.io' (note the swapped 'r' and 'o'). The system actually validates this misspelled value as the prefix key in specific edge cases or the constraint is testing deviation detection from the actual 'notifications.argoproj.io'.

trigger_keywords: conditional output based on object presence
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the condition object contains a key named 'oncePer', the variable's evaluated value is appended to the end of the output line with an '=' separator. If the key is absent, no such suffix is added.

This generated package must cover the full detailed-PRD behavior represented by all authoritative public test case files, not just a subset mentioned in the fuzzy PRD. Public feature files: feature1_message_templating, feature2_1_condition_firing, feature2_2_once_per_grouping, feature3_destination_resolution, feature4_1_add_subscription, feature4_2_remove_subscription, feature4_3_annotation_keys. Feature descriptions include: Render a notification message from a named message template. The caller supplies a set of named templates (each providing a message body written in a curly-brace placeholder syntax), a list of template names to apply in order, and a map of variables. The engine evaluates the selected template's body against the variables and returns the rendered message text. Placeholders are replaced by the corresponding variable values, including nested field access via dotted paths, and a standard library of text helper functions (for example, upper-casing a value) is available through a pipe operator. The output is the single line 'message=' followed by the fully rendered text.; Evaluate the firing conditions of a trigger. A trigger holds an ordered list of conditions; each condition carries a boolean expression (written in a small expression language over the supplied variables) and a list of template names to send when it fires. Given the conditions and a variable map, the engine evaluates each condition's expression in order and reports, per condition, whether it fired, which template names it would send, and its grouping value (empty here). A condition fires only when its expression evaluates to boolean true; an expression that evaluates to a non-true value, or that fails to evaluate (for example because it references a variable that is absent), yields a not-fired result rather than an error. Each condition produces one output line of the form 'triggered=<bool> templates=<comma-separated> oncePer=<value>', in condition order.; Compute a condition's de-duplication grouping value. In addition to its firing expression, a condition may declare a 'oncePer' expression whose evaluated result is used to ensure a notification is only delivered once per distinct value (for example, once per deployment revision). When such an expression is present, the engine evaluates it against the supplied variables and reports the resulting value as a string alongside the firing result. The expression may be a bare variable reference or a richer lookup such as indexing into a nested map by key. The output line carries the fired flag, the templates to send, and the resolved grouping value in 'oncePer='.; Resolve notification destinations from subscription annotations. Subscriptions are expressed as annotation keys of the form '<prefix>/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>' whose value is the recipient. The resolver expands every subscription into a mapping from trigger name to a list of (service, recipient) destinations, applying these rules: when a subscription names an explicit trigger, that trigger is used verbatim and any default triggers are ignored; when a subscription omits the trigger (key shaped as '<prefix>/subscribe.<service>'), the destination is attached to each of the caller-supplied default triggers, unless a per-service default trigger list is provided for that service, in which case those service-specific triggers are used instead. Each resulting destination is emitted as a line 'trigger=<t> service=<s> recipient=<r>', ordered by trigger then by service and recipient.; Add a recipient to a subscription, expressed as an annotation entry. Given an existing annotation map, a trigger, a service, and one or more recipients, the operation records the subscription under the annotation key '<prefix>/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>'. If the key does not yet exist it is created with the new recipient(s); if it already exists, each new recipient is appended to the existing...

The execution adapter must accept exactly the input shapes used by public_test_cases and emit stdout exactly equal to each expected_output. Observed top-level input fields/values include: action=['annotation_key', 'format_notification', 'get_destinations', 'run_trigger', 'subscribe', 'unsubscribe'], templates=['{dict}'], apply=['[list]'], vars=['{dict}'], conditions=['[list]'], annotations=['{dict}'], defaultTriggers=['[list]'], trigger=['my-trigger', 'on-deploy'], service=['email', 'slack'], recipients=['[list]'], recipient=['my-channel1'], prefix=['test.prefix']. Do not require a different discriminator field, different action names, JSON output where line output is expected, or extra PASS/FAIL metadata.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, numeric formatting, and deterministic serialization. If prose, fuzzy_prd, context pointers, or older hidden constraints are vague or conflict with expected_output, the public_test_cases expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

Ignore any vague analogy or context pointer that names an unrelated module, pipeline, login flow, audit generator, or previous project. Such wording is non-normative; only this repo’s detailed PRD, FULL_SCOPE, EXECUTION_CONTRACT, and public_test_cases define the task.

context_pointers.vague_reference: follow the deletion protocol described in the subscription cleanup module for empty lists
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The constraint refers to the specific logic in the `cleanup_subscriptions` function where if the resulting recipient array length equals zero, the function calls `delete_annotation` to remove the key entirely from the state object.

context_pointers.vague_reference: order by the hierarchical keys in the destinations map
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The sorting algorithm traverses the internal `destinations` map sequentially, extracting `trigger`, `service`, and `recipient` from the stored annotation key strings in that exact order for primary, secondary, and tertiary sorting.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more detail on that — check the codebase or the existing test cases for the exact behavior.

feature1_message_templating.json.description: Render a notification message from a named message template. The caller supplies a set of named templates (each providing a message body written in a curly-brace placeholder syntax), a list of template names to apply in order, and a map of variables. The engine evaluates the selected template's body against the variables and returns the rendered message text. Placeholders are replaced by the corresponding variable values, including nested field access via dotted paths, and a standard library of text helper functions (for example, upper-casing a value) is available through a pipe operator. The output is the single line 'message=' followed by the fully rendered text.

feature2_1_condition_firing.json.description: Evaluate the firing conditions of a trigger. A trigger holds an ordered list of conditions; each condition carries a boolean expression (written in a small expression language over the supplied variables) and a list of template names to send when it fires. Given the conditions and a variable map, the engine evaluates each condition's expression in order and reports, per condition, whether it fired, which template names it would send, and its grouping value (empty here). A condition fires only when its expression evaluates to boolean true; an expression that evaluates to a non-true value, or that fails to evaluate (for example because it references a variable that is absent), yields a not-fired result rather than an error. Each condition produces one output line of the form 'triggered=<bool> templates=<comma-separated> oncePer=<value>', in condition order.

feature2_2_once_per_grouping.json.description: Compute a condition's de-duplication grouping value. In addition to its firing expression, a condition may declare a 'oncePer' expression whose evaluated result is used to ensure a notification is only delivered once per distinct value (for example, once per deployment revision). When such an expression is present, the engine evaluates it against the supplied variables and reports the resulting value as a string alongside the firing result. The expression may be a bare variable reference or a richer lookup such as indexing into a nested map by key. The output line carries the fired flag, the templates to send, and the resolved grouping value in 'oncePer='.

feature3_destination_resolution.json.description: Resolve notification destinations from subscription annotations. Subscriptions are expressed as annotation keys of the form '<prefix>/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>' whose value is the recipient. The resolver expands every subscription into a mapping from trigger name to a list of (service, recipient) destinations, applying these rules: when a subscription names an explicit trigger, that trigger is used verbatim and any default triggers are ignored; when a subscription omits the trigger (key shaped as '<prefix>/subscribe.<service>'), the destination is attached to each of the caller-supplied default triggers, unless a per-service default trigger list is provided for that service, in which case those service-specific triggers are used instead. Each resulting destination is emitted as a line 'trigger=<t> service=<s> recipient=<r>', ordered by trigger then by service and recipient.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=trigger-a service=slack recipient=my-channel
trigger=trigger-b service=slack recipient=my-channel
trigger=trigger-c service=slack recipient=my-channel

feature4_1_add_subscription.json.description: Add a recipient to a subscription, expressed as an annotation entry. Given an existing annotation map, a trigger, a service, and one or more recipients, the operation records the subscription under the annotation key '<prefix>/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>'. If the key does not yet exist it is created with the new recipient(s); if it already exists, each new recipient is appended to the existing recipient list unless it is already present, and the recipients are stored as a single value joined by semicolons. The output lists the resulting annotation entries as '<key>=<value>' lines, sorted by key.

feature4_2_remove_subscription.json.description: Remove a recipient from a subscription annotation entry. Given an existing annotation map, a trigger, a service, and a recipient, the operation locates the subscription annotation for that trigger and service and removes the named recipient from its recipient list. If recipients remain after removal, the annotation entry is kept with the updated value; if the removed recipient was the last one, the annotation entry is deleted entirely. The output lists the remaining annotation entries as '<key>=<value>' lines, sorted by key; when the entry was deleted and no annotations remain, the output is empty.

feature4_3_annotation_keys.json.description: Derive the canonical annotation keys used by the subscription scheme. All keys are built from a configurable prefix that defaults to a fixed namespace and can be overridden. The subscribe key for a given trigger and service has the shape '<prefix>/subscribe.<trigger>.<service>'. The notified key, used to record that a notification has already been delivered, has the shape 'notified.<prefix>'. When a prefix override is supplied it is applied before deriving both keys. The output reports the two derived keys, one per line, as 'subscribe_key=<value>' and 'notified_key=<value>'.

_constraints.output_rule: Output must start with 'message=<rendered_value>'
When a template name is missing, return an error line first, then a failure line for the specific template.
Output line format where condition evaluated to true.
Output line format where condition evaluated to false.
If 'oncePer' key exists in condition, append '=evaluated_value' to the output line.
If 'oncePer' key is absent, the output line must NOT contain 'oncePer='.
When subscription has an explicit trigger, use that trigger and return one destination line.
When subscription lacks a trigger, apply the destination to every default trigger.
When a per-service default trigger list is provided for the specific trigger in the subscription, use those specific triggers instead of the global default triggers.
Construct key as '<prefix>/subscribe.<t>.<s>'. If key exists, append recipients separated by ';' (duplicates removed). If new, create key.
Single recipient is stored as is.
Remove matching recipient. If list becomes empty, delete annotation entirely (output empty or no lines).
Output 'subscribe_key=<computed>' and optionally 'notified_key=<computed>'.
If no prefix provided, use 'notifications.argoproj.io'.
Supports nested field access via dotted paths (e.g., `{{.a.b.c}}`) even if intermediate objects don't exist in simple scalar tests.
Supports text helper functions via pipe operator (e.g., `{{.state | upper}}`).
If condition fails to evaluate due to missing variable, treat as non-true (do not fire). No error thrown.
Default namespace used is 'notifications.argoproj.io' unless prefix argument overrides.
Output lines must be ordered by trigger name first, then by service name, then by recipient.
since only one action is provided, output lines must be organized appropriately.
CANNOT include history in constraints analysis.

_constraints.input_condition: All constraints MUST be outputted as JSON
_constraints.output_rule: Do not add timestamps.
Constraint must be included in returned JSON.
Constraint must be outputted in JSON format.
Constraint logic must be included in all constraints.
